planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring after frost.

directions: The dramatic near-black young foliage of this hybrid catalpa matures to dark green, and white summer flower panicles follow on older trees. Plant in full sun and moist, deep soil, sheltered from wind to protect the large leaves. Hard pruning in spring intensifies the purple new growth if flowers are not the priority.

planting.note: Sow seed shallowly in spring after the last frost.

directions: Sow the fine seed just below the surface in a warm, moist bed and keep evenly damp until seedlings appear. This compact Asian catalpa bears creamy-yellow flowers marked orange and purple, followed by slender bean-like pods. Plant in full sun in average soil, and prune in late winter to shape the broad rounded crown.

planting.note: Sow seed shallowly in spring once soil warms.

directions: Surface-sow the light seed in a warm bed and keep it evenly moist through germination. This Chinese species is admired for its abundant pink to rosy-lilac flowers spotted with red-brown, showier than most catalpas. Grow in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil, sheltered from wind to guard the foliage and blooms.

planting.note: Sow seed shallowly in spring after frost.

directions: Sow the fine seed just beneath the surface in warm, moist soil and thin the seedlings as they grow. This smaller catalpa forms a rounded head of glossy leaves and bears white flowers spotted purple in early summer. Plant in full sun and average, well-drained soil, and shape lightly in late winter.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings out after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Surface-sow the dust-fine seed on warm, moist mix and do not cover it, as light aids germination. This extremely fast grower is prized for timber and for its upright panicles of pale violet trumpet flowers in spring. Plant in full sun and deep, well-drained soil, and note it can be coppiced for giant leaves and rapid regrowth.

planting.note: Move seedlings outdoors after frost once soil is warm.

directions: Sprinkle the tiny seed on the surface of warm, moist mix and keep it bright and humid until sprouts appear. This paulownia opens fragrant violet-blue trumpet flowers before the large leaves expand in spring. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, sheltered from wind that can snap its soft, fast-grown wood.

planting.note: Plant dormant grafted stock in early spring.

directions: This vigorous, fast-establishing scholar tree develops an oval crown and flowers at a younger age than seedlings. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, watering through the first summers to build the deep root system. Creamy-white pea flowers cover the canopy in late summer, followed by beaded green pods.

planting.note: Plant the grafted standard in spring.

directions: A strongly weeping form usually top-grafted onto a standard, its stiff pendulous branches arching to the ground. Plant in full sun and well-drained soil and stake the leader if a taller cascade is wanted. It rarely flowers and is grown chiefly for its sculptural winter silhouette and arbor-like summer canopy.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring after the last frost.

directions: Nick and soak the seed, then sow about half an inch deep in gritty, well-drained soil. This small southern-plains native bears drooping clusters of pink pea flowers in spring, followed by distinctive black beaded pods that resemble a string of beads. Plant in full sun to part shade where it tolerates heat, drought, and alkaline soils once established.

planting.note: Set out balled or containerized trees in early spring once the soil can be worked.

directions: Dig a hole no deeper than the root ball and twice as wide, keeping the root flare at grade. This male, fruitless selection needs little pruning and tolerates pavement, pollution, and heat once established. Water deeply through the first two summers, then enjoy the uniform golden fall color that drops almost overnight.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a narrow, upright form suits tight streetscapes.

directions: Position with the root flare visible and firm soil gently around the ball. Its tight, tapering habit fits narrow spaces and needs almost no corrective pruning. This fruitless male form gives clean golden autumn foliage without messy seed drop.

planting.note: Best transplanted in spring into deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant with the flare at soil level and backfill with native soil, watering it in well. Known for a strong central leader and neatly rounded crown, this male selection stays fruitless. Distinctive skirt-like leaves turn clear yellow before dropping in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a border, container, or small courtyard where its slow habit is an asset.

directions: Set the root ball at grade in loose, free-draining soil and mulch lightly. This compact, slow-growing dwarf forms a dense vase of butterfly-shaped leaves that hold gold color in fall. Little pruning is needed beyond removing any crossing shoots in dormancy.

planting.note: Transplant in spring as a symmetrical street or lawn tree.

directions: Plant at the same depth it grew in the nursery and stake only if needed on exposed sites. Valued for an evenly upright, symmetrical crown, this male clone avoids fruit entirely. Expect reliable golden fall color and excellent tolerance of urban conditions.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, deep soil where its spiky-fruit-free habit is welcome.

directions: Dig a wide, shallow hole and set the tree with its flare exposed, then water thoroughly. This selection has rounded leaf lobes and sets little to no of the notorious spiny gumball fruit. Fall foliage turns burgundy and purple, especially after cool nights.

planting.note: Set out in spring in deep, moisture-retentive soil for the strongest autumn display.

directions: Plant at grade in fertile soil and mulch to keep the roots cool and evenly moist. This English selection is prized for deeply cut, narrow-lobed leaves that color orange, red, and purple. Give it room to develop its pyramidal-to-oval crown and prune only for structure while young.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a vertical accent or narrow screen.

directions: Firm the soil around the ball at grade and water in; the extremely narrow habit needs almost no lateral space. Multiple plants set close together make a tall living column or screen. Star-shaped leaves flush green and turn rich red-purple in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a rounded shrub or small standard in a border.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil; often grafted as a standard, it forms a dense, mounded head. Foliage emerges glossy green and turns purple, red, and orange late in the season. Water regularly through establishment and prune lightly to keep the ball shapely.

planting.note: Plant in spring where added cold hardiness is valued.

directions: Set the root flare at grade in deep soil and water deeply during the first seasons. This cold-hardy, fast-growing selection makes an upright, symmetrical shade tree. Its glossy leaves turn burgundy-red in fall, holding late into the season.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings in spring; sow stratified seed in a cold frame for propagation.

directions: Cold-stratify seed for one to two months, then sow shallowly and grow on before transplanting at flare level. Its three-lobed, maple-like leaves emerge with a bronze tint and color to red and purple in autumn. Give it space and steady moisture in a warm, sheltered site.

planting.note: Plant young trees in spring in a warm, sheltered position.

directions: Sow stratified seed in a cold frame, or set out nursery trees at grade in spring. Slower and smaller than American sweetgum, it forms a rounded, bushy crown of small five-lobed leaves. Autumn color runs from orange to soft red; the bark is the source of storax resin.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow from stratified seed or set out container trees at flare level, watering to establish. New growth flushes plum-purple, and the three-lobed leaves color richly in autumn. A refined Chinese species that appreciates shelter from harsh, drying winds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moisture-retentive, acidic soil for the best fall color.

directions: Sow stratified seed or transplant young trees at grade, resenting root disturbance once established. Prized for brilliant fall color, its leaves emerge red-tinged and turn flaming scarlet and orange in autumn. Keep the soil consistently moist and slightly acidic in a sheltered, sunny spot.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, acidic soil where the red new growth shows off.

directions: Set at grade in moist, acidic soil and mulch to keep roots cool; avoid disturbing the taproot. This selection flushes new leaves bright red through the season, then colors scarlet and burgundy in fall. Water regularly, especially in the first years and during summer dry spells.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a tidy, upright street or lawn tree.

directions: Plant with the flare exposed and water deeply while it establishes a strong root system. This uniform, upright selection has glossy summer leaves that turn brilliant fire-engine red in autumn. It tolerates a range of soils but grows best where moisture is dependable.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a broadly pyramidal shade tree is wanted.

directions: Set the root ball at grade and keep evenly moist through the establishment years. This selection has a strong central leader, symmetrical pyramidal form, and lustrous dark green summer leaves. Fall brings dependable deep red color across the whole canopy.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a cascading specimen in moist, sheltered soil.

directions: Plant at grade in rich, moist soil and shelter from hot afternoon sun and drying wind. Cascading branches form a mounding, waterfall-like dome of rounded blue-green leaves. Autumn foliage turns apricot-yellow and gives off a sweet, burnt-sugar scent as it fades.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil with shelter from harsh sun.

directions: Set the root flare at grade and keep the soil consistently moist, especially in the first summers. This upright selection has purple-bronze new foliage that matures to bluish-green. Fall color shifts through bronze and gold with the characteristic caramel fragrance.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into cool, moist woodland soil.

directions: Sow stratified seed on the surface of a moist mix, or plant young trees at grade in humus-rich soil. Smaller and more shrubby than the common katsura, it bears larger, heart-shaped leaves. Fall color turns clear yellow with the same sweet, cotton-candy fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, moist soil where the golden foliage catches light.

directions: Set the root ball at grade in moisture-retentive soil and keep well watered, as it dislikes drought. This selection holds bright golden-yellow, feathery foliage through the growing season. The soft needles turn russet-bronze before dropping in fall from this deciduous conifer.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a small weeping accent in moist soil.

directions: Plant at grade in consistently moist soil; often staked to establish a leader before cascading. This dwarf, weeping form makes trailing curtains of soft blue-green needles. The deciduous foliage turns orange-bronze in autumn before dropping to reveal graceful branch lines.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, fertile soil with room for a large canopy.

directions: Set the flare at grade in deep, well-drained soil and water through establishment. This selection edges each lobed leaf with a bright yellow margin that softens as summer advances. Cup-shaped, orange-banded green flowers appear high in the canopy once trees mature.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a tall, narrow form fits a confined space.

directions: Plant at grade in deep soil and water well; the upswept branches keep the crown narrow. This columnar form suits avenues and tighter sites than the broad species. Tulip-shaped green-and-orange flowers open high in the canopy on mature trees in late spring.

planting.note: Sow acorns in fall or set out container plants in spring for hedging.

directions: Plant acorns an inch deep, or space young shrubs closely if training a screen or hedge. This dense evergreen oak from East Asia takes shearing well and tolerates coastal wind and salt. Its small, glossy, finely toothed leaves and shrubby habit mark an established plant.

planting.note: Set out this hybrid oak as a container tree in fall or early spring.

directions: Plant a container-grown tree with the root flare at grade and water deeply during establishment. This natural cross of live oak and overcup oak grows faster than either parent and holds semi-evergreen foliage. Its broad, spreading crown and vigorous growth mark a maturing shade tree.

planting.note: Set out container plants in spring or fall in a spot with room for a dense crown.

directions: Plant with the root ball level with the soil surface and mulch to keep roots cool and moist. This compact, upright southern magnolia has dark leaves with rich rust-brown felt beneath, making it good for tight spaces. Large fragrant white flowers open through summer once the plant is established.

planting.note: Plant container or B&B trees in spring or fall with room for a broad pyramid.

directions: Set the tree at its original planting depth and water regularly through the first two summers. This vigorous, fast-growing southern magnolia forms a symmetrical pyramid and blooms at a relatively young age. Big, creamy, lemon-scented flowers appear from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant this narrow selection where a slim upright evergreen is wanted.

directions: Plant at grade in well-drained soil and keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist while it establishes. This tightly columnar southern magnolia suits screens, corners, and small lots where width is limited. Fragrant white flowers open in summer against glossy dark green leaves.

planting.note: Give this large selection ample room for a full pyramidal crown.

directions: Set the tree at its nursery depth and water deeply until the roots reach out into surrounding soil. This dense southern magnolia carries very large leaves and produces some of the biggest flowers of any selection. Enormous fragrant white blossoms open across summer.

planting.note: Plant this compact heavy bloomer where its flowers can be enjoyed up close.

directions: Plant at grade and mulch well, keeping the soil consistently moist during establishment. This smaller southern magnolia flowers heavily even as a young plant and can be kept espaliered or shrubby. Large, richly fragrant white flowers appear over a long summer season.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a spot sheltered from early-morning sun after frost.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil where late frosts are less likely to catch the early flowers. This deciduous Chinese magnolia blooms on bare branches before its leaves emerge. Cup-shaped, ivory-white, lightly fragrant flowers cover the tree in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich soil with shelter from harsh wind.

directions: Plant at grade in fertile, evenly moist soil and protect the young canopy from drying wind. This deciduous hybrid was among the first clear-yellow magnolias and forms a neat pyramid. Soft primrose-yellow, lightly fragrant flowers open in spring as the leaves begin to unfurl.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the plant at grade in acidic soil and keep it evenly moist while roots establish. This narrow, pyramidal hybrid holly holds fine-textured leaves and fruits heavily even without a nearby male. Small spring flowers precede masses of bright red berries that last through winter.

planting.note: Set out young trees after the last frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of a warm, well-drained mix and press in lightly without covering. Transplant seedlings while young to avoid disturbing the taproot, and give the tree full sun and open space. Foliage releases a strong lemon scent when crushed, and small creamy flower clusters appear in the crown on mature trees.

planting.note: Plant out in spring after frost danger passes in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on a gritty mix at warm temperatures and keep just moist until sprouts appear. Grow on in full sun with sharp drainage, as this compact gum resents wet feet. Expect showy scarlet-to-pink flower clusters in summer followed by large woody gum nuts.

planting.note: One of the hardiest eucalypts, it can be set out once the ground is workable in spring.

directions: Cold-stratify the seed for a few weeks, then surface-sow and keep cool and moist to trigger germination. Plant the young tree in full sun with free-draining soil where it can develop its striking mottled cream-and-grey bark. Clouds of white blossom open in summer and the tree tolerates hard winter frost.

planting.note: A very cold-tolerant gum that can go out in spring once soil warms slightly.

directions: Surface-sow seed after a short chilling period and keep lightly moist until seedlings emerge. Grow in full sun and moist but well-drained soil, and prune hard in spring if you want to keep the rounded juvenile leaves. Small white flowers appear in summer, and the blue-grey foliage is prized for cutting.

planting.note: Set out after frost as it grows fast and needs room to spread.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of a warm, well-drained mix and pot on promptly as growth is rapid. Plant in full sun with ample space, since this species reaches great size quickly. Large single white flowers open in winter and spring, and the silvery juvenile leaves are strongly aromatic.

planting.note: Hardy enough to plant out in spring in most temperate gardens.

directions: Chill the seed briefly, then surface-sow and keep cool and evenly moist until germination. Grow in full sun with good drainage, and coppice regularly to keep the round clasped juvenile leaves that spin in the wind on dead stems. White flowers appear in summer on unpruned older growth.

planting.note: Frost-tender and tropical, so plant only where nights stay warm.

directions: Sow the tiny seed on warm, moist media and keep humidity high until seedlings establish. Plant in full sun with rich, consistently moist soil in a frost-free location. The famous multicolored bark peels to reveal streaks of green, orange, and purple, with small white flowers on mature trees.

planting.note: Needs very sharp drainage and full sun, best planted after frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed on a very gritty mix and water sparingly to avoid damping off. This sprawling mallee demands full sun and fast-draining, low-fertility soil to thrive. It bears huge red flowers up to three inches across against silvery grey leaves, followed by oversized woody nuts.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, dry, well-drained position.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of a lean, gritty medium and keep barely moist at warm temperatures. Give this small mallee full sun and dry, alkaline-tolerant soil. Bright red bud caps pop off to release large yellow flowers, followed by distinctive ribbed fruit.

planting.note: Tolerant of seasonal flooding, plant out in spring near water or open ground.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on warm, moist media and prick out seedlings early. This wide-spreading gum handles both drought and periodic waterlogging once established. Masses of small white flowers attract bees in summer, and the smooth bark sheds in patches of cream, grey, and pink.

planting.note: A tough windbreak tree best planted out in spring after frost.

directions: Sow seed on a warm, well-drained mix and keep lightly moist until sprouting. Plant in full sun where its drought resistance suits dry, exposed sites and shelterbelts. Cream flowers draw pollinators in summer, and the mottled yellow-brown bark and dense canopy make it a durable shade tree.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Surface-sow seed on a gritty medium and water carefully to prevent rot. This small mallee wants full sun and fast-draining soil and tolerates coastal salt spray. Pendulous red bud caps and fruit resembling fuchsia flowers hang along the stems for much of the year.

planting.note: A large slow tree best planted out in spring with room to grow.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of a warm, well-drained mix and grow seedlings on before planting out. Give this broad tree full sun and space, as it develops a spreading canopy and rough bark. Large clusters of cream to pink flowers feed bees and birds, followed by the big urn-shaped nuts known as honky nuts.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in an open, sunny position with good drainage.

directions: Surface-sow seed on warm media and transplant seedlings while young to preserve the root system. Plant in full sun with free-draining soil where its tall straight trunk and dappled grey bark can be showcased. White flowers open in winter and attract nectar-feeding birds.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Pour near-boiling water over the hard seed and soak overnight to break dormancy before sowing warm. Plant this fast-growing small tree in full sun with sharp drainage, and stake young stems if exposed to wind. Fragrant golden ball flowers smother the branches in late winter and spring, giving Australia its national floral emblem.

planting.note: A hardy timber wattle that can be planted out in spring.

directions: Scarify or hot-water treat the seed, then sow warm and keep moist until it sprouts. Grow in full sun to light shade in deep, moist soil where it can reach tree size. Pale cream flower balls appear in spring, and the prized dark timber develops in the sturdy trunk over years.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Soak the seed in hot water overnight and sow into warm, gritty media. This tough small tree or large shrub thrives in full sun with dry, well-drained soil and shrugs off drought. Triangular blue-grey phyllodes line the arching branches, which carry bright yellow flower balls in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with free-draining soil.

directions: Pretreat the hard seed with a hot-water soak, then sow warm and keep just moist. This graceful weeping shrub or small tree wants full sun and good drainage and tolerates dry conditions. Slender grey-green foliage drapes the branches, and pale lemon flower spikes appear from autumn through winter.

planting.note: Fast-growing tree best planted out in spring with room to spread.

directions: Treat the seed with hot water to break its coat, then sow warm and grow on quickly. Plant in full sun in most soils, where this vigorous tree establishes fast. Fragrant pale yellow flower balls appear in late spring, and the bark has long been a source of tannin.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Scarify the hard seed and soak before sowing into warm media. This thorny small tree loves full sun, heat, and dry, well-drained ground once established. Intensely fragrant golden puffball flowers appear in late winter and spring and have long been used in perfumery.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in full sun; tolerates heavy clay and drought.

directions: Hot-water treat the seed and sow warm, keeping media only lightly moist. This elegant tree tolerates full sun, heavy soils, and dry inland conditions. Its silvery weeping foliage gives strong ornamental value, and pale yellow flower balls appear irregularly through the year.

planting.note: A hardy suckering wattle that can be planted out in spring.

directions: Soak the hard seed in hot water overnight and sow into warm, gritty media. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, allowing space for its suckering, thicket-forming habit. Slender blue-grey foliage sets off masses of bright yellow flower balls in late winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Pretreat seed with hot water and sow warm, keeping evenly moist until germination. This graceful small tree grows quickly in full sun or dappled shade with reasonable drainage. Arching branches of fine green foliage carry soft pale yellow flower spikes in spring.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in full sun with well-drained soil.

directions: Hot-water treat the seed to soften the coat, then sow warm and keep lightly moist. Plant this soft-foliaged small tree in full sun in average, free-draining soil. Tiny fringed leaves give a fine texture, and fragrant lemon-yellow flower balls cover the plant in spring.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Soak the seed in hot water overnight and sow into warm, gritty media. This arching shrub or small tree wants full sun and sharp drainage and tolerates dry sites. Ferny, feathery green foliage arches gracefully and is smothered in golden flower balls in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in full sun with well-drained soil.

directions: Sow the winged seed shallowly in warm media and be patient, as germination can be uneven. Plant in full sun with free-draining soil where the tall pyramidal form has room to develop. Golden-orange comb-shaped flowers cover the tree in spring, above ferny grey-green foliage.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in full sun with deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Handle the irritating seed hairs with gloves, then soak and sow the seed in warm media. Plant in full sun in deep soil where the tall trunk can develop over years. Mature trees drop their leaves and erupt in dense clusters of scarlet bell flowers in early summer.

planting.note: A coastal tree best planted in spring in full sun; tolerates salt spray.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed on warm, moist media and keep it from drying out. Plant in full sun in a coastal or mild garden, where it withstands wind and salt. Crimson brush-like flowers cover the spreading canopy in early summer, giving the tree its Christmas association.

planting.note: Frost-tender, so plant rooted cuttings only once nights are reliably warm.

directions: Take a thick stem cutting, let the cut end callus for a week, then plant it in fast-draining, barely moist media to root. Grow in full sun with sparing water, keeping it dry through winter dormancy. Clusters of intensely fragrant white, yellow, or pink flowers open at the branch tips through the warm months.

planting.note: Set out balled or containerized trees in early spring so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained spot with the root flare level with the surrounding grade. The blue-green needles and orange upper-bark scaling develop best in poor, sandy soils rather than rich ground. Cones ripen over two seasons; a mature tree begins bearing pollen catkins and small cones after roughly a decade.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into open ground where the broad crown will have room to spread.

directions: Choose a full-sun site with room for a dense, dark-green pyramidal crown that later flattens with age. This tough species tolerates road salt, clay, and coastal exposure once established. Water regularly the first two seasons, then only in prolonged drought.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in gritty, well-drained soil for foundation or rock-garden use.

directions: Set this low, mounding pine at grade in sharply drained soil and full sun. Pinch the soft spring candles by half to keep growth compact and dense. It shrugs off cold and poor soil but resents wet feet and heavy shade.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil where its layered, twisting form can be admired.

directions: Give this refined, slow-growing pine full sun and good drainage, keeping the root flare above soil level. The short, blue-green, twisted needles and tiered branching make it a favorite for specimen and bonsai use. Prune only lightly by candle-pinching to shape the picturesque crown.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, well-drained soil; the narrow column is slow but very hardy.

directions: Set out in full sun in moist but well-drained ground, allowing space for a dense, narrowly pyramidal habit. This alpine species grows slowly and holds a tidy shape with almost no pruning. Seeds require a long cold stratification, so nursery stock is the practical route for most gardeners.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil; tolerates heat, drought, and alkaline ground.

directions: Plant in full sun on almost any well-drained soil, including chalky or rocky sites. The stiff dark needles and neat conical outline make it a durable, low-maintenance specimen. Young cones show a distinctive blue-violet color before ripening to brown.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, well-drained soil where the tall trunk has vertical room.

directions: Give this stately western pine full sun and deep, sharply drained soil. The furrowed bark famously smells of vanilla or butterscotch when warmed. It is very drought-tolerant once rooted and needs little care beyond removing damaged limbs.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a dry, well-drained slope with full exposure.

directions: Plant in full sun in dry, well-drained soil typical of chaparral foothills. This species produces the heaviest cones of any pine, so site it away from paths and parking. Water sparingly once established, as it is highly drought-adapted.

planting.note: Plant in spring in hot, dry, well-drained ground; extremely heat- and drought-tolerant.

directions: Site in full sun on lean, fast-draining soil where summers are hot and dry. The airy, gray-green foliage casts light shade and the forked trunks give a distinctive open silhouette. Large edible-seeded cones follow years of growth; give it plenty of room to develop.

planting.note: Plant in spring in coastal or mild-climate gardens with sandy, fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant in full sun in loose, sandy soil, ideally in a coastal setting where fog supplements rainfall. This rare California native tolerates wind and salt spray but dislikes prolonged wet cold. Given open irrigation it grows faster and taller than its stunted wild bluff form.

planting.note: Plant in spring in coastal gardens; tolerant of wind, fog, and poor soils.

directions: Choose a breezy, full-sun coastal site with fast-draining soil. The serotinous cones cling to the branches for years and open after fire or heat. It establishes quickly as a windbreak or screen in mild maritime climates.

planting.note: Plant in spring on dry, rocky, low-fertility ground in full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun on poor, dry, well-drained soil where little else thrives. The tight, hooked serotinous cones remain closed on the trunk and branches for decades until fire releases the seed. It is fast-growing and useful for revegetating harsh, rocky sites.

planting.note: Plant in spring in warm-temperate gardens with deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this Himalayan-foothill pine full sun and deep, fast-draining soil in a frost-mild climate. The long, drooping, bright-green needles and thick fire-resistant bark are distinctive. It is drought-hardy once established and grows quickly in warm conditions.

planting.note: Plant in spring in warm, dry-climate gardens with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant in full sun in fast-draining soil in a frost-free or nearly frost-free region. The long, soft, drooping needles and columnar habit make it a striking street and park tree. It is remarkably drought- and heat-tolerant and can resprout after fire, unusual among pines.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sandy coastal soils where salt and wind are common.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, sandy, well-drained soil, ideal for stabilizing dunes and coastal ground. The long, stout needles and large glossy cones give it a bold texture. It withstands salt spray, wind, and drought once established.

planting.note: Plant in spring in lean, sharply drained soil at full exposure; very slow-growing.

directions: Site in full sun in poor, gritty, fast-draining soil, avoiding rich or wet ground. The dense, resin-flecked needles and gnarled form make it a prized dwarf-scale specimen. Growth is extremely slow, so this pine rewards patience in rock gardens and troughs.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil with shelter from harsh wind.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, well-drained soil sheltered from drying wind. The long, blue-green, gracefully drooping needles and pendulous resin-coated cones make it an elegant specimen. Give it space, as the sweeping lower branches often reach the ground.

planting.note: Plant in spring in warm-temperate gardens on acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun on acidic, fast-draining soil in a mild climate. This fast-growing Chinese pine bears slender, flexible needles and reddish young bark. It tolerates poor red-clay soils and is widely used for reforestation and screening.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, well-drained soil, giving the broad crown room to spread. The long, soft, slightly drooping needles and large resinous cones with edible seeds are ornamental features. It grows at a moderate pace and needs little pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil where the exfoliating bark can be seen up close.

directions: Site this slow-growing pine in full sun and well-drained soil near a path or patio where its bark is a focal point. The trunk flakes to a mottled patchwork of gray, green, and creamy white, resembling a sycamore. Often multi-stemmed, it can be limbed up over time to display the striking bark.

planting.note: Plant in spring on sandy, dry, acidic soils in cold northern gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun on lean, sandy, acidic ground where hardiness matters more than form. The short, twisted needles and curved serotinous cones are typical of this pioneering northern species. It grows quickly on poor sites and tolerates extreme cold.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sandy, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained sandy loam, avoiding heavy or wet clay. The reddish plated bark and paired brittle needles that snap cleanly are identifying traits. This cold-hardy timber pine forms a straight trunk and open, oval crown with age.

planting.note: Plant in spring in mild climates with fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, well-drained soil in a frost-mild region. The long, thin, bright-green needles hang gracefully, giving the whole tree a soft weeping look. It grows fast and is widely planted for both ornament and forestry in warm climates.

planting.note: Plant in spring in mild-winter gardens with deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this bold Mexican pine full sun and deep, well-drained soil in a frost-mild climate. The very long, drooping blue-green needles form dramatic tufted rosettes on stout branches. It appreciates summer moisture and grows into a broad, picturesque crown.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dry, well-drained soil in warm southwestern gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun on dry, fast-draining soil in a warm-temperate to subtropical highland climate. Unusual among pines, it can resprout from the base after fire or cutting. The slender gray-green needles and small cones that take three years to mature are distinctive.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil in warm southwestern highland gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in dry, well-drained soil in a mild-winter region. Young trees pass through a grass-like stage with very long needles before forming a trunk, much like longleaf pine. The huge, coarse needle tufts make it a striking specimen once established.

planting.note: Plant in warm, frost-free tropical or subtropical sites with sandy soil.

directions: Plant in full sun in sandy, well-drained soil in a frost-free tropical climate. This fast-growing pine tolerates poor coastal savanna soils and periodic flooding. It is widely grown for timber and resin and forms a tall, straight trunk with an open crown.

planting.note: Plant in spring in hot, dry Mediterranean-type gardens with fast drainage.

directions: Plant in full sun in dry, well-drained soil in a hot Mediterranean-type climate. It is highly drought-tolerant and thrives on rocky, low-fertility ground. The bright-green needles and reddish bark make it a sturdy screen or shade tree in arid regions.

planting.note: Plant in spring in warm gardens on well-drained soil; drought-tolerant once rooted.

directions: Plant in full sun in dry, fast-draining soil in a mild-winter region. This adaptable Mexican pine grows quickly and holds clustered serotinous cones along its branches. It tolerates drought and poor soil and is often used for shelterbelts and reforestation.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dry, rocky, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun on lean, dry, sharply drained soil typical of high desert slopes. This slow-growing pinyon bears needles usually in bundles of four and produces edible pine nuts. Water sparingly, as it is exceptionally drought-adapted once established.

planting.note: Plant in spring on dry, rocky slopes with full sun and fast drainage.

directions: Plant in full sun on dry, gritty, well-drained soil in a region with cold, dry winters. The flaking gray-green bark resembles lacebark pine, and the large cones yield prized edible chilgoza nuts. Growth is slow, and mature nut-bearing trees take many years to develop.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring in well-drained soil where the umbrella crown can be admired.

directions: Set this multi-stemmed, grafted cultivar in full sun and well-drained soil, keeping the flare above grade. The numerous upright trunks fan out into a flat-topped, umbrella-like crown with attractive orange-red bark. It stays compact and needs only light candle-pinching to maintain its rounded form.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring in well-drained soil in full sun for best needle color.

directions: Plant this grafted cultivar in full sun on well-drained soil to bring out its banded foliage. Each needle carries two yellow bands, so a cluster viewed end-on shows concentric rings resembling a dragon's eye. Give it good air circulation and light pruning to keep an open, layered habit.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring in full sun and sharp drainage for a compact specimen.

directions: Set this slow, mounding grafted cultivar in full sun and well-drained soil. The dark-green needles and prominent silvery-white winter buds that resemble billowing thunderheads are its signature. Pinch candles in spring to build density and keep the compact, irregular form.

planting.note: Plant grafted or cutting-grown stock in spring in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this very dwarf, bun-shaped mugo in full sun and sharply drained soil at grade. It forms a tight, rounded green globe that needs almost no pruning to stay neat. Ideal for rock gardens, troughs, and small foundation plantings where space is limited.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring in moist, well-drained soil with room to cascade.

directions: Plant this grafted weeping cultivar in full sun to light shade in moist, well-drained soil. Its long, soft blue-green needles hang from cascading branches that drape and mound irregularly. Staking a central leader when young controls the ultimate height and shape.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring in well-drained soil and full sun for best blue color.

directions: Set this grafted cultivar in full sun and well-drained soil to develop its strong silvery-blue color. The short, twisted needles and open, tiered branching give a refined, sculptural look. Light candle-pinching maintains its picturesque, layered habit over many years.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring in well-drained soil where a narrow vertical accent is wanted.

directions: Plant this narrow, upright cultivar in full sun and well-drained soil as a slender vertical accent. The blue-green needles and tight, columnar habit suit small gardens and formal plantings. Occasional light shearing keeps the profile crisp, and it tolerates poor, sandy soil.

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring in well-drained soil in full sun for a dense specimen.

directions: Plant this compact, slow-growing selection in full sun and well-drained soil. Bright silvery-white candles open in spring against the dense dark-green needles for strong seasonal contrast. It holds a tidy pyramidal shape and tolerates heat, salt, and urban conditions.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant this low, spreading form of mugo pine in full sun and sharply drained soil. It stays broad and prostrate, making a rugged groundcover for slopes, rock gardens, and foundations. Pinch spring candles to control size, and avoid soggy sites that invite root rot.

planting.note: Set out young trees in early spring once the ground has thawed and drained.

directions: Plant the root ball level with the surrounding soil in a deep, well-drained site with room for a large tree. Water steadily through the first few summers while the taproot establishes, then let this fast-growing spruce fend for itself. Its long pendulous cones and drooping branchlets develop as the tree matures over many years.

planting.note: Transplant nursery stock in spring into loose, moisture-retentive ground.

directions: Dig a wide, shallow hole and settle the plant so the crown sits just above grade to keep the flat top dry. The signature nest-like depression in the center forms naturally as the horizontal branches spread outward. Give it consistent moisture the first two seasons and shear only lightly to preserve the cushioned shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its spring cones can be admired up close.

directions: Set the graft union at or slightly above soil level in a sunny, well-drained bed. This selection is prized for the rosy-red immature cones it hangs from branch tips each spring, which mature to brown by autumn. Stake young plants if you want a more upright leader, as the habit is naturally irregular and spreading.

planting.note: Use as a narrow vertical accent; plant in spring in deep soil.

directions: Plant this tight, upright form where a slim evergreen column is wanted, allowing at least five feet of width at maturity. The dense ascending branches shed snow better than most spruces, making it a good choice in northern gardens. Water regularly while establishing and no pruning is needed to keep the narrow silhouette.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a slender weeping specimen with a staked leader.

directions: Position in full sun with the central leader staked upright, since the branches cascade tightly against the trunk. The remarkably narrow footprint suits small yards and courtyard plantings. Keep the roots evenly moist through the first summers and remove the stake only once the trunk holds itself vertical.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this slow, dense strain tolerates wind and cold.

directions: Plant this hardy, compact form of white spruce in an open sunny spot where it can serve as a windbreak or specimen. Its slow growth and naturally dense pyramid need almost no shaping. Water through establishment, after which it handles drought and prairie winters with ease.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a broad, silvery-blue pyramid.

directions: Set out this grafted selection in full sun to bring out the intense powder-blue needle color. It holds a dense, broadly conical shape with a naturally strong central leader and needs no staking. Water deeply during dry spells while young and avoid soggy ground, which the roots dislike.

planting.note: Plant in spring; regarded as one of the bluest spruces.

directions: Plant in a bright, open location to maximize the striking silver-blue foliage this cultivar is famous for. Young trees can grow somewhat irregularly, so lightly guide the leader for a symmetrical form. Provide even moisture through the establishment years and keep the site well drained.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a compact blue pyramid.

directions: Plant this seed-strain blue spruce in full sun where its uniform conical habit and blue needles can shine. It stays smaller and denser than the wild species, fitting mid-sized landscapes well. Water regularly the first two seasons and mulch to keep the root zone cool.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low, rounded blue mound.

directions: Plant this dwarf globe in full sun near the front of a border or in a large container. It forms a flattened bun of silvery-blue needles that broadens slowly with age. Shear any errant upright shoots to keep the rounded outline and water during summer dry spells.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact blue pyramid or mound.

directions: Site this compact dwarf in full sun to deepen its rich blue color. It grows as a broad cone or mound and can be pruned to a small pyramid over time. Keep the soil evenly moist while establishing and ensure sharp drainage to prevent root problems.

planting.note: Plant in spring; stake to set the height of the weeping form.

directions: Plant this weeping selection of the Colorado blue spruce in full sun and stake the leader to whatever height you prefer. The powder-blue branches drape downward in an informal cascade, so each plant develops a unique silhouette. Water through establishment and give it well-drained ground for best color and health.

planting.note: Plant in spring where a narrow blue upright column is wanted.

directions: Use this narrow, upswept blue spruce as a vertical accent in tight spaces or flanking an entry. The ascending branches stay close to the trunk, giving a distinctly columnar profile with silvery-blue needles. Water while establishing and no pruning is required to maintain the slim shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring with some afternoon shade in hot regions to prevent burn.

directions: Plant this golden-needled spruce where morning sun lights the foliage but harsh afternoon rays are softened, since intense heat can scorch the tips. The short, dense needles glow butter-yellow on the exposed side and green within, on a slow, narrow pyramid. Keep evenly moist and mulch well, as this cultivar dislikes drought stress.

planting.note: Plant in spring to enjoy the golden spring flush of new growth.

directions: Plant in full sun where the striking creamy-yellow new shoots can be seen each spring before they mature to deep green. This forms a dense, classic pyramid with the fine short needles typical of the species. Water steadily through establishment and provide well-drained soil sheltered from drying winds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, moist, sheltered site for best results.

directions: Give this elegant weeping spruce a cool, humid, sheltered location resembling its native Siskiyou mountains. Long curtains of foliage hang vertically from the branches once the tree matures, an effect that takes years to develop. Keep the soil consistently moist and avoid hot, dry, or windy exposures that stress it.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a tiny rock-garden or trough bun.

directions: Tuck this miniature blue-green bun into a rock garden, trough, or the front of a bed where its slow, tidy growth suits small spaces. It forms a dense flattened globe that gains only an inch or two a year. Water during dry weather and give it a cool spot with sharp drainage.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a very narrow weeping vertical accent.

directions: Plant this extremely narrow weeping form where a tall, slim silhouette is needed, staking the leader upward. The branches sweep downward with two-toned green-and-silver needles typical of Serbian spruce. It adapts to a wide range of soils and city conditions, needing only even moisture while young.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a large open site with shelter from spring frost.

directions: Give this majestic species ample room in deep, moist, well-drained soil and shelter from late frosts that can damage new growth. Its exceptionally long, drooping needles and pendulous branchlets make it one of the most graceful large spruces. Water well while establishing and keep the site open and sunny.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool climate with reliable moisture.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy East Asian spruce in a cool site with steady moisture and good drainage. The flattened needles show bright silvery bands beneath, giving the canopy a shimmering quality in a breeze. Water during establishment and avoid hot, exposed positions, which it tolerates poorly.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a large garden or arboretum setting.

directions: Plant this handsome Chinese spruce in deep, well-drained soil with room to spread into a broad pyramid. It is admired for the abundant reddish-purple young cones that stud the branches before ripening. Water through establishment and give it full sun in a sheltered, cool-summer location.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact rounded specimen.

directions: Plant this slow dwarf where its dense, rounded-to-conical form and short yellow-green needles can be appreciated up close. Over decades it may develop several irregular leaders, giving it character in a mixed conifer bed. Water while establishing and site it in full sun with good drainage.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low, spreading cushion.

directions: Plant this low, spreading dwarf in the foreground of a border or over a wall edge where it can mound broadly. The layered branches form a flat-topped cushion of rich green needles. Give it full sun, water during establishment, and prune only to remove the occasional stray shoot.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a trough, rock garden, or fairy garden.

directions: This true miniature makes a tight green bun ideal for troughs, rock gardens, and container groupings. It grows barely an inch a year and forms a slight central depression as it broadens. Keep the roots evenly moist, provide sharp drainage, and shelter it from reflected heat.

planting.note: Plant in spring and stake to control the ultimate height.

directions: Train this strongly weeping cultivar to whatever height you choose by staking the leader, then let the branches spill downward. Without a stake it sprawls to form a mounding groundcover of cascading dark-green foliage. Water through establishment and grow in full sun with well-drained soil.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this popular Caucasian fir in deep, fertile soil that stays moist but drains freely. Its glossy dark-green needles hold well, which is why it is widely grown as a premium Christmas tree and landscape specimen. Water steadily while establishing and shelter young trees from late spring frosts.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, moist mountain-like setting.

directions: Give this tall European species deep, moist, well-drained soil and ample space to reach its full stately height. The flattened needles show two silvery bands beneath, and the tree keeps a narrow spire when young. Water through establishment and protect from drought and hot, dry winds.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates heat and limy soils better than most firs.

directions: Plant this distinctive fir in full sun in well-drained soil, including alkaline sites that many firs resent. Its short, stiff, radially arranged needles give the branches a bottlebrush look unlike any other fir. Water while establishing, after which it handles heat and some drought comparatively well.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the strongest blue color.

directions: Plant this blue-needled selection of Spanish fir in full sun and well-drained soil to bring out the striking silvery-blue color. The stiff radial needles create the same bottlebrush texture as the species but in a cooler hue. Water through establishment and give it a warm, open position tolerant of chalky ground.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool climate with moist, gritty soil.

directions: Plant this narrow-spired mountain fir in a cool site with moist, well-drained soil similar to its high-elevation home. Its dense, silvery blue-green foliage and tight steeple shape make it distinctive in northern gardens. Keep the roots cool and moist and avoid hot, humid lowland conditions it dislikes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, well-drained site for the corky bark and blue needles.

directions: Plant this Southwestern variety of subalpine fir for its thick, soft, whitish corky bark and intensely blue needles. It forms a narrow pyramid and prefers a cool, well-drained position out of drying winds. Water through establishment and keep the root zone cool with mulch.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact blue pyramid for smaller gardens.

directions: Plant this slow, compact selection where its dense powder-blue foliage and neat pyramidal form suit a smaller landscape. It offers the striking blue color of corkbark fir at a fraction of the size. Give it a cool, well-drained spot, water while establishing, and mulch to keep roots cool.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the curled silvery needles can be seen closely.

directions: Plant this celebrated fir in full sun in moist, well-drained soil where its upcurved needles reveal bright silver undersides. Even young plants produce showy upright violet-blue cones along the branches. Water steadily through establishment and shelter it from harsh, drying exposures.

planting.note: Plant in spring; among the more pollution-tolerant firs.

directions: Plant this adaptable Japanese fir in moist, well-drained soil where it forms a broad, sturdy pyramid. The needles are dark green above with silvery bands beneath, and it tolerates urban air better than many firs. Water while establishing and give it an open, sunny position.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the most heat- and humidity-tolerant fir for the South.

directions: Plant this vigorous Japanese fir where its rare tolerance of heat, humidity, and heavy soils makes it one of the few firs for the Southeast. The stiff, sharp-tipped needles form a coarse, bold-textured canopy. Water through establishment and provide full sun with reasonable drainage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, moist site; one of the faster-growing firs.

directions: Plant this fast-growing Japanese fir in cool, moist, well-drained soil where it forms a shapely pyramid. The soft needles are notched at the tip and gleam silvery-white beneath, brightening the canopy. Water steadily while establishing and keep it out of hot, dry situations.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, moist, humid maritime climate.

directions: Give this Pacific Northwest native the cool, moist, humid conditions of its coastal-mountain home for best growth. The lustrous dark-green needles are densely set and pleasantly fragrant when crushed. Water through establishment, keep roots cool and moist, and avoid hot inland exposures.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the brightest silver-blue foliage.

directions: Plant this outstanding blue selection of white fir in full sun where the long, upswept, silvery-blue needles glow. It keeps the heat and drought tolerance of the species, making it one of the more adaptable blue conifers. Water while establishing and provide well-drained soil in an open site.

planting.note: Plant in spring as an irregular blue dwarf for small spaces.

directions: Plant this compact, slow-growing white fir where its irregular, informal shape and long blue needles add character to a small bed or rock garden. It develops a picturesque, somewhat unpredictable outline with age. Water through establishment, give it full sun, and prune lightly only to correct wayward growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, high-drainage mountain-like site.

directions: Plant this stately western fir in a cool site with excellent drainage, echoing its Sierra Nevada origins. The four-sided blue-green needles curve upward and the tree carries large, erect barrel-shaped cones near the top. Water while establishing and avoid hot, poorly drained lowlands.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates dry, limy soils and warm summers.

directions: Plant this adaptable Mediterranean fir in full sun and well-drained soil, including dry and alkaline sites many firs cannot handle. The stiff, spreading, sharp-pointed needles radiate around the shoots, and it withstands heat better than most. Water while establishing, though be aware early growth can be nipped by late frosts.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerant of dry and chalky soils.

directions: Plant this North African fir in full sun and well-drained soil, where it tolerates drought and lime better than most firs. The short, dense, dark-green needles crowd the shoots and give the tree a lush, full appearance. Water through establishment and give it a warm, open position.

planting.note: Plant in spring with light shade in hot climates to prevent scorch.

directions: Plant this low, spreading golden dwarf where morning sun brings out the yellow needles but hot afternoon light does not scorch them. It grows slowly into a broad, flat-topped mound that brightens rock gardens and mixed beds. Keep evenly moist, mulch the roots, and shelter it from harsh winter sun.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact, fragrant globe for small gardens.

directions: Plant this dwarf balsam fir where its dense, rounded form and short, aromatic dark-green needles suit a rock garden or foundation planting. It stays low and mounded, releasing the classic balsam scent when brushed. Provide cool, moist, well-drained soil, water while establishing, and give it full sun to light shade.

planting.note: Plant in spring; adaptable and among the more heat-tolerant firs.

directions: Plant this adaptable East Asian fir in moist, well-drained soil where it forms a broad, dense pyramid. Its glossy green, sharp-tipped needles lack the silvery undersides of many firs, giving a uniformly rich green canopy. Water through establishment; it tolerates heat and a range of soils better than most firs.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants in spring or early fall so roots establish before extreme heat or cold.

directions: Plant at the same depth it grew in the nursery pot and firm the soil around the shallow root mass. Space plants five to six feet apart on a slope or bed and let the silver-blue mat knit together to smother weeds. Water through the first summer, then rely on rainfall in all but severe drought.

planting.note: Install in spring once soil can be worked, giving low mats a full season to spread.

directions: Dig a hole twice as wide as the root ball but no deeper, and backfill with the native soil. Mulch lightly to hold moisture while the plant knits into a dense, powder-blue carpet that turns plum-tinged in cold weather. Prune only to remove wayward tips, since old bare wood does not regrow.

planting.note: A tough coastal selection best set out in spring in fast-draining ground.

directions: Choose a sunny, well-drained spot, since this juniper tolerates sand and salt spray but sulks in wet clay. Set plants so the crown sits level with the surrounding grade and water in well. The feathery gray-green foliage takes on a purple flush after the first hard frosts.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for the fullest, most even mound.

directions: Space plants about four feet apart to form a low, layered mound of soft gray-green branches. Keep the root zone free of standing water and mulch to suppress weeds during establishment. Foliage turns a distinct rich purple through winter, then greens up again in spring.

planting.note: A very cold-hardy prairie selection; plant in spring for a season of establishment.

directions: Set this flat-growing juniper at grade in full sun and space plants five to six feet apart for solid coverage. It hugs the ground tightly, making an excellent living mulch on banks. The bright green summer foliage takes on a purplish cast in cold winters.

planting.note: Give full sun to keep the golden color bright; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed where the vase-shaped, chartreuse-gold foliage can stand out against darker greens. Backfill with native soil, water in, and avoid shade, which dulls the color. The tips deepen to bronze-orange as fall arrives.

planting.note: A golden sport of Blue Rug; plant in full sun in spring for the brightest color.

directions: Set plants at grade in well-drained soil and full sun to keep the foliage a vivid gold. Space four to five feet apart so the low, trailing mat closes over the ground. Winter cold brings out orange and bronze tones in the trailing branch tips.

planting.note: Plant in spring in lean, well-drained soil for the tightest silver-blue mat.

directions: Dig a shallow, wide hole and set the root ball level with the soil surface. This selection hugs the ground even more tightly than Blue Rug, forming a dense silver-blue carpet. Winter turns the foliage a soft plum-purple before it recovers in spring.

planting.note: A slow, mounding dwarf; plant in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in a sunny spot with sharp drainage, keeping the crown at grade. This compact bun of steely silver-blue needles grows slowly and rarely needs pruning. Water during dry spells the first year, then only in drought.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for a fast, low silver-blue mat.

directions: Set plants three to four feet apart in well-drained soil and full sun. The low, wide-spreading branches carry soft, intensely blue-gray foliage that makes a striking groundcover. Trim overlong shoots in spring to keep the mat even.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to enjoy the contrast of gold tips over blue foliage.

directions: Give this spreading juniper full sun and sharp drainage so the new growth glows sulfur-yellow against the older blue foliage. Plant at grade and water through the first season. Light spring shearing keeps the bicolor effect fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; the semi-upright form benefits from occasional thinning.

directions: Set this irregular, upright-spreading juniper in full sun with good air movement to reduce dieback on inner branches. Plant at grade and remove any dead interior twigs each spring. The stiff, ascending shoots carry striking gray-blue needles.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; excellent over walls and in rock gardens.

directions: Plant this dwarf, mounding groundcover at grade in well-drained soil and full sun. The layered branches spill attractively over rocks and low walls and root as they trail. Foliage is blue-green in summer and tinged purple in cold weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; naturally dense pyramid needs little shaping.

directions: Plant this broad, teardrop-shaped juniper in full sun with room to reach its mature width. Set the root ball level with grade and water regularly the first year. Its naturally tidy, blue-green cone rarely needs pruning beyond tip trims.

planting.note: A fast columnar; space for screening and plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Space plants three to five feet apart for a dense privacy screen or plant singly as a formal accent. Set at grade in full sun and keep watered while establishing. The rich dark green column holds its shape and needs only occasional shearing.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; the picturesque twisted habit is a landscape focal point.

directions: Give this sculptural juniper full sun and space to show its irregular, twisting branches. Plant at grade in well-drained soil and water through the first season. Prune only to enhance the natural windswept character, never to force a formal shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for a graceful fountain-shaped shrub.

directions: Plant in full sun with room for the arching, fountain-like branches to spread. Set at grade, water while establishing, and give it space rather than shearing it into a ball. The mint-green foliage deepens slightly in cold weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to hold the golden-bronze color year round.

directions: Set this compact spreader in full sun so the gold color stays bright rather than fading to green in shade. Plant level with grade and water through the first summer. It keeps its warm bronze-gold tone through winter, unlike many golden junipers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the deepest golden new growth.

directions: Plant this low, compact spreader in full sun where the lacy golden foliage can shine. Set at grade in well-drained soil and water while establishing. Color intensifies in cool weather and stays gold through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and allow room for the arching fountain habit.

directions: Give this vigorous, arching juniper full sun and plenty of horizontal space. Plant at grade and water regularly the first year, then only in drought. The bright mint-green foliage holds its color all year with minimal pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; a durable, salt-tolerant coastal groundcover.

directions: Set this low, wide-spreading juniper at grade in full sun and well-drained soil, including sandy coastal sites. Space four to five feet apart to form a durable mat. The dense blue-green foliage tolerates salt spray and wind well.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good air circulation.

directions: Plant this broad, spreading juniper in full sun with room for its layered blue-green branches. Set at grade and provide good air movement to limit tip blight. Water while establishing, then only during extended dry spells.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the brightest star-shaped golden foliage.

directions: Plant this low, wide spreader in full sun so the golden, star-patterned foliage keeps its color. Set the root ball at grade and water through the first season. It stays compact and rarely needs pruning beyond stray-tip removal.

planting.note: A large old-fashioned spreader; give it plenty of room and full sun.

directions: Plant this vigorous, wide-spreading juniper where its arching branches can extend ten feet or more. Set at grade in full sun and water while establishing. Avoid crowding it against walkways, since it resents hard pruning into old wood.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to keep the golden tips vivid.

directions: Give this broad, arching juniper full sun so the new golden-yellow growth stays bright. Plant at grade in well-drained soil and allow generous horizontal space. The gold tips fade to yellow-green as the season cools.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the most intense golden color.

directions: Plant this spreading juniper in full sun so the feathery foliage holds its deep golden color into winter. Set at grade and water through the first summer. Its lower, more compact habit suits foundation beds and slopes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the bicolor gold-and-blue effect.

directions: Set this low, spreading juniper in full sun so the golden new growth stands out over the blue-green interior. Plant at grade in well-drained soil and water while establishing. The frosted gold tips are brightest in spring and early summer.

planting.note: A very narrow column; plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant this pencil-thin column in full sun with excellent drainage, since it dislikes wet feet. Space two to three feet apart for a vertical accent row. In snowy regions, tie the branches loosely to prevent splaying under heavy snow.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; tighter and more upright than Skyrocket.

directions: Set this narrow blue column in full sun with sharp drainage and space two to three feet apart for a living wall. It holds its tight form with almost no pruning. The steel-blue foliage keeps its color through winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for a broad silver-blue pyramid.

directions: Plant this broad pyramidal juniper in full sun and well-drained soil, giving it space to reach its mature width. Set at grade and water through the first season. The bright silver-blue color is strongest in full sun.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for a dense, broad silvery-blue pyramid.

directions: Give this dense, upright juniper full sun and room to develop its broad pyramid. Plant at grade in well-drained soil and water while establishing. The frosty silver-blue foliage is exceptionally full from the ground up.

planting.note: A slow, very hardy narrow selection for cold, dry climates; plant in spring.

directions: Plant this slow, narrow column in full sun and sharp drainage, ideal for cold and windy sites. Space two to three feet apart for a compact screen. Its tight, blue-green form needs virtually no pruning.

planting.note: Often staked as a standard; plant in spring in full sun for the fullest drape.

directions: Plant this weeping juniper in full sun where its long, cascading, string-like branches can hang freely. Stake the leader if you want an upright weeping standard. Set at grade, water the first year, and let the graceful drape develop naturally.

planting.note: A wide, low spreader; plant in spring in full sun with room to expand.

directions: Plant this broad, low-spreading juniper in full sun where its soft silver-gray foliage can billow. Set at grade in well-drained soil and water while establishing. Female plants bear small frosty-blue berries that attract birds.

planting.note: A narrow native column often used as a Skyrocket alternative; plant in spring.

directions: Plant this tall, narrow native column in full sun and well-drained soil for a dense vertical accent. Space three to four feet apart for a screen. Its silvery blue-green form is more disease-resistant than many exotic columnar junipers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; noted for heavy blue berry set on females.

directions: Plant this broad pyramidal native tree in full sun with room to reach its full size. Set at grade in well-drained soil and water through the first season. Female plants produce abundant silvery-blue berries that draw cedar waxwings and other birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; holds green color well through winter.

directions: Plant this narrow, upright native in full sun and well-drained soil as a screen or specimen. Set at grade and water while establishing. Unlike many red cedars it keeps a rich green through winter and sets heavy blue berries on female plants.

planting.note: A tough, low mounding spreader; plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant this low, mounding groundcover in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing five to seven feet apart. It forms a dense, soft green mat that resists the tip blight common to some savins. Water while establishing, then it is highly drought tolerant.

planting.note: A very low, feathery spreader; plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Set this flat, feathery groundcover at grade in full sun and space five to six feet apart. It stays lower than most savins and holds a bright green color into winter. Water the first season, then rely on its strong drought tolerance.

planting.note: An extremely hardy, low, wide mat; plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant this very cold-hardy, low-growing juniper in full sun, spacing six to seven feet apart for full coverage. It forms a soft green, feathery carpet that stays lower and greener than the species. Water while establishing, then it thrives on neglect.

planting.note: A classic tiered low spreader; plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant this layered, low-spreading juniper in full sun and well-drained soil for a tiered, horizontal effect. Set at grade and space six to eight feet apart. Provide good air circulation to reduce the tip blight this old cultivar can be prone to.

planting.note: Sow cleaned seed in fall so winter cold breaks dormancy, or set out nursery plants in spring.

directions: This tough limestone-country native tolerates thin, rocky, alkaline soils where little else grows. Sow cleaned seed in fall for natural stratification, expecting slow and uneven germination, or plant nursery-grown trees in spring. Once established it needs no supplemental water and forms dense, drought-proof cover.

planting.note: Set out balled or container plants in spring or early fall while soil is cool and moist.

directions: Plant with the root flare at grade in evenly moist, well-drained soil, keeping the crown out of standing water. Water deeply through the first two seasons and mulch to hold moisture, since dry roots cause browning. The species matures into a broad pyramid and begins bearing small upright cones after several years.
